original_abstract: Implementing efficient carbon dioxide reduction into value-added chemicals requires the development of catalytic materials that are selective, stable, and composed of earth-abundant elements. In this study, we demonstrate that nanostructured thin films of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) serve as powerful, atomically defined platforms for the electrocatalytic reduction of CO₂ to CO in aqueous media. By systematically screening various metal-porphyrin building blocks integrated into an aluminum-based framework, we identified the cobalt-functionalized MOF, Al₂(OH)₂TCPP-Co, as an exceptionally effective catalyst. Optimized MOF thin films, fabricated via atomic layer deposition-mediated synthesis, achieved a Faradaic efficiency for CO production of 76% and sustained stability for over seven hours with a per-site turnover number (TON) of 1400. In situ spectroelectrochemical analysis revealed that the majority of the cobalt active sites within the porous landscape are redox-accessible, undergoing a Co(II) to Co(I) reduction that drives the catalytic cycle. This work establishes MOFs as a versatile class of heterogeneous electrocatalysts that bridge the gap between molecular precision and robust material architecture for sustainable energy applications.
humanized_abstract: To transform CO2 into value-added chemicals and fuels, highly efficient, stable, and affordable catalysts are needed. Here, we show that nanostructured thin films of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) can be used as powerful, atomically defined catalysts for the electrochemical reduction of CO2 to CO in solution. By exploring a range of metal-porphyrin-based linkers embedded in an aluminum-based MOF structure, we found that thin films of Co-functionalized MOF (Al2(OH)2TCPP-Co) showed outstanding performance. By optimizing their preparation via atomic layer deposition-mediated synthesis, we obtained films with a Faradaic efficiency (FE) of 76% for CO2 reduction and long-term stability of over 7 h, corresponding to a site-based turnover number (TON) of 1400. In situ spectroelectrochemical measurements revealed that most of the Co sites within the MOF film are accessible for reduction, indicating that the CO2 reduction reaction occurs at the Co2+/1 redox couple. This study confirms that MOFs could serve as a new family of heterogeneous electrocatalysts, where their molecular diversity can be fully preserved at the material level.
